nativeRange.habitat: Pelagic open ocean (free-floating holopelagic forms) and attached to hard substrate in shallow coastal reef zones; intertidal to subtidal
nativeRange.biotope: Tropical and subtropical open-ocean drift communities and shallow reef zones

avoidWith: heavy herbivorous grazers that may consume it before it establishes

summary: Pseudo-Kelp is a large brown macroalga (Sargassum sp.) that mimics the look of true kelp in reef displays, with distinctive gas-filled bladders called pneumatocysts that keep its fronds upright. It is available in branching and plating growth forms with differing bladder presence and growth rates.
careNotes:
Pseudo-Kelp belongs to the genus Sargassum, a group of brown macroalgae (class Phaeophyceae, order Fucales, family Sargassaceae) notable for their kelp-like stature and distinctive pneumatocysts — small, spherical, gas-filled bladders that keep fronds buoyant and upright. In the wild, holopelagic species such as S. natans and S. fluitans drift freely across the Sargasso Sea and Atlantic, forming the Great Atlantic Sargassum Belt, a phenomenon that intensified dramatically from 2011 onward as large blooms became an annual event tied to elevated nutrient loading from land. Aquarium-suitable Sargassum are typically holdfast-forming species that attach to live rock or rubble in the display tank rather than drifting freely.

Three trade forms are commonly available under the Pseudo-Kelp umbrella. The typical Pseudo-Kelp has an upright, branching structure with pneumatocysts and a yellow to gold to brown coloration. Zig-Zag Kelp shares the same pneumatocyst anatomy but has a thinner, zig-zag branching structure and a similarly sparse overall habit. Plating Gulfweed is a notably different morph that grows in a flat, plating fashion with a defined central midrib and does NOT form air bladders; it is described as fairly small and slow-growing, and may represent a distinct species from the other two forms. Species identification for all three is left at genus level (Sargassum sp.) as the exact species are not confirmed.

For the typical Pseudo-Kelp and Zig-Zag Kelp forms, high lighting is generally advised, consistent with the genus' preference for sun-exposed surface waters. Plating Gulfweed similarly prefers high lighting but grows considerably more slowly than the upright forms. All three forms tend to appear sparse rather than bushy in captivity.

Sargassum is generally considered suitable forage for herbivorous tangs and surgeonfish. A tank of 120 litres or more is advisable to give the specimen room to reach meaningful size and to buffer grazing pressure from tankmates.

Propagation is typically achieved by fragmentation — detaching a branch and anchoring it to rockwork. Attachment methods include superglue, zip-ties or fishing line, or wedging the holdfast into a crevice in live rock. **Do not bury the base in a sand substrate — this species will MELT at the base if buried.** Attach to rock rather than placing the holdfast in sand. Water parameters follow standard tropical reef values.
breedingNotes: Propagation in aquariums is generally achieved through fragmentation of the thallus (the main body of the alga). Sexual reproduction in Sargassum follows an alternation of generations — a lifecycle in which a spore-producing form and a gamete-producing form alternate — but this process has not been documented as a practical propagation pathway in hobby settings.

primary.name: Mosaic Macros
primary.notes: Primary trade source for this entry. Confirms Sargassum sp. identification, pneumatocyst morphology, yellow-to-brown coloration, holdfast attachment habit, and small-cutting availability. No numeric water parameters provided.

additional.name: Mosaic Macros — Zig-Zag Kelp
additional.notes: Second Mosaic SKU consolidated into this entry. Confirms zig-zag branching structure, pneumatocysts, sparse/not-bushy habit. No numeric water parameters.

additional.name: Mosaic Macros — Plating Gulfweed
additional.notes: Third Mosaic SKU consolidated into this entry. Notably: plating growth form, defined midrib, no pneumatocysts, fairly small size, slow growth rate, high lighting. May represent a distinct species from the other two SKUs.

additional.name: Wikipedia — Sargassum
additional.notes: Taxonomy cross-check: confirms order Fucales; confirms pneumatocyst biology, Sargasso Sea origin, and 2011+ Great Atlantic Sargassum Belt events. Not a husbandry source.

additional.name: Mosaic Macros general macroalgae care guide
additional.notes: Slice-wide secondary source — provides authoritative Mosaic-wide parameter targets (temp 22-26°C, pH 8.1-8.4, Ca 400-450 ppm, Mg 1250-1350 ppm, NO3 5-20 ppm, PO4 0.02-0.1 ppm) and the 4-method propagation standard. Added retroactively 2026-06-08.
